cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure out the answer to this question. The question is asking what is right about happy, with the options being (A) Happy is a gift, (B) Happy is limited, (C) Happy is contagious, and (D) Happy is no more than a skill. First, I'll look at the context provided. The story starts with a man on the 70th floor of the Empire State Building considering jumping. The narrator uses a scenario of restored sight to make the man consider staying alive, highlighting that appreciating what you have (like eyesight) makes other troubles seem less significant. The story then moves into three misconceptions about happiness. The first misconception is that knowing the tools for happiness will make it work like magic. The text argues against this by saying it requires effort and practice, similar to learning a new skill. The second misconception is that being content would reduce motivation. The story counters this by saying happy people are actually more energetic and ambitious. The third part of the story isn't fully provided, but the last paragraph talks about sourpusses being a public danger and being happy as part of being considerate. This implies that happiness affects others positively, and being unhappy can negatively impact those around you. This is pointing towards the idea that happiness is contagious. Looking at the options, (C) Happy is contagious seems to align with the last paragraph. The story suggests that being in a good mood is important not just for yourself but for others around you, just as a sour mood can spoil things.
